# **The Hilal Dashboard: Technical Architecture and Mathematical Foundations for Lunar Visibility Prediction**

The determination of the Islamic lunar calendar represents a sophisticated intersection of ancient religious practice and modern high-precision astronomy. For centuries, the beginning of sacred months-most notably Ramadan, Shawwal (for Eid al-Fitr), and Dhu al-Hijjah (for Eid al-Adha)-has been contingent upon the physical sighting of the new crescent moon, or hilal, at sunset.1 In the contemporary digital landscape, the requirement for predictive accuracy has led to the development of computational frameworks that move beyond simple age-based models to integrate bi-parametric astronomical criteria, atmospheric physics, and real-time meteorological data.3 This report details the technical architecture of "The Hilal Dashboard," an interactive platform designed to bridge the gap between high-precision data science and cultural tradition through the use of local astronomical calculations and transparent mathematical models.4

## **Foundations of Lunar Visibility Modeling**

The core challenge in predicting the first sighting of the lunar crescent is that the moon must be bright enough and the sky dark enough for the eye to distinguish the two.6 At the moment of the astronomical new moon, or conjunction, the moon and sun share the same geocentric ecliptic longitude, rendering the moon entirely invisible from Earth.8 Visibility only becomes a probabilistic possibility as the moon moves further away from the sun's glare, creating a narrow sliver of reflected light.9 This transition involves a complex interplay of geometric variables calculated at the precise moment of best visibility following sunset.11

### **Geometric and Topocentric Variables**

To move away from "black-box" APIs, the Hilal Dashboard utilizes the Skyfield library to calculate a suite of fundamental variables locally. These calculations prioritize topocentric coordinates, which account for the observer's specific position on the WGS84 ellipsoid of the Earth, rather than geocentric coordinates calculated from the Earth's center.12 The difference between geocentric and topocentric elongation can be as much as one degree, which is a critical margin in predicting borderline sightings.10

| Variable | Mathematical Context | Significance in Visibility |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Arc of Light (![][image1]) | ![][image2] | The raw angular separation between sun and moon centers. |
| Arc of Vision (![][image3]) | ![][image4] | The altitude difference between the moon and the center of the setting sun. |
| Relative Azimuth (![][image5]) | $ | az\_m \- az\_s |
| Topocentric Width (![][image6]) | ![][image7] | The thickness of the lit crescent in arcminutes. |
| Moon's Age (![][image8]) | ![][image9] | The time elapsed since the birth of the new moon. |
| Lag Time (![][image10]) | ![][image11] | The duration the moon remains above the horizon after sunset. |

Historical data indicates that single-parameter models based solely on age or lag time are unreliable.3 A moon that is 24 hours old may remain invisible if its ecliptic latitude is unfavorable, while a younger moon at a high ecliptic latitude may be seen.3 Modern predictive engines therefore rely on polynomials that combine ![][image3] and ![][image6] to estimate the contrast ratio between the lunar disk and the twilight sky.6

## **The Mathematical Core: Polynomial Visibility Criteria**

The Hilal Dashboard implements the two most rigorous empirical criteria recognized by the international astronomical community: the Yallop Criterion and the Odeh Criterion.3 These models serve as the engine for generating global visibility heatmaps and local "sight-ability" scores.11

### **Implementation of the Yallop Criterion**

Bernard Yallop's 1997 method, developed for the HM Nautical Almanac Office, provides a single parameter, ![][image12], representing the "ease of visibility".6 This parameter is derived from a cubic polynomial that models the minimum ![][image3] required for a given crescent width ![][image6].19 The dashboard calculates ![][image12] using the following expression:

![][image13]  
The variables ![][image3] and ![][image6] must be calculated at the "Best Time" (![][image14]), defined as approximately 4/9ths of the lag time after sunset.11 The resulting value allows the dashboard to classify any location into one of six distinct visibility zones.11

| Zone Code | Range of q | Interpretation and Requirements |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| A | ![][image15] | Easily visible to the unaided eye. |
| B | ![][image16] | Visible under perfect atmospheric conditions. |
| C | ![][image17] | Optical aid may be needed to find the crescent. |
| D | ![][image18] | Visible only with binoculars or telescope. |
| E | ![][image19] | Not visible with conventional telescopes. |
| F | ![][image20] | Impossible (below the Danjon limit). |

Analysis of these zones reveals that locations falling into Zone C or D often provide the most significant point of contention in traditional sighting committees.20 The dashboard’s transparency ensures that users understand why a specific city might be marked as "conditionally visible," a distinction that often depends on whether they are using binoculars or the naked eye.3

### **Implementation of the Odeh Criterion**

The Odeh Criterion, developed by Mohammad Odeh in 2004, utilizes a larger database of 737 sightings, many of which were collected through the Islamic Crescent Observation Project (ICOP).3 Unlike Yallop, Odeh's model uses airless topocentric values for ![][image3], essentially stripping away the effects of refraction during the initial calculation phase to ensure a more standardized geometric baseline.3

The Odeh ![][image21] value is calculated as follows:

![][image22]  
The dashboard maps ![][image21] to a simplified set of visibility zones that emphasize the transition between digital imaging and human vision.15

| Odeh Zone | Range of V | Sighting Expectation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Zone A | ![][image23] | Visible by naked eye. |
| Zone B | ![][image24] | Visible by optical aid; possibly by naked eye. |
| Zone C | ![][image25] | Visible by optical aid only. |
| Zone D | ![][image26] | Not visible even with optical aid. |

The Hilal Dashboard provides a consensus view by overlaying these two models. In regions where Yallop and Odeh diverge, the dashboard marks the area as a "Zone of Uncertainty," acknowledging that local variations in eye sensitivity and atmospheric transparency become the dominant factors.21

## **The Physics of the Horizon: Refraction and Extinction**

To achieve observatory-grade accuracy, the dashboard must account for the bending of light at the horizon, which significantly alters the apparent position of the moon.23 This requires the implementation of advanced atmospheric models that respond to local weather conditions.23

### **Modeling Atmospheric Refraction**

Atmospheric refraction is the result of light passing through layers of air with increasing density as it approaches the Earth's surface.26 This effect is zero at the zenith but reaches its maximum at the horizon, where the air appears to "lift" celestial objects by approximately 34 arcminutes-enough to make the sun or moon appear fully above the horizon when they are geometrically below it.25

The dashboard utilizes Saemundsson’s inverse formula for converting true altitude (![][image27]) to apparent altitude (![][image28]) 23:

![][image29]  
This base value of ![][image30] (in arcminutes) is then corrected for local temperature (![][image31] in Celsius) and atmospheric pressure (![][image32] in hPa) using the approximate factor 23:

![][image33]  
The inclusion of this physics-based correction is vital for the "Horizon View" Simulator. Without it, the simulated moon would appear lower than its actual observed position, leading to failed sighting attempts.24 The dashboard dynamically fetches ![][image31] and ![][image32] data from the OpenWeatherMap API to refine this calculation in real-time for the user's specific GPS coordinates.27

### **Atmospheric Extinction and Contrast**

Visibility is ultimately a contrast problem: ![][image34], where ![][image35] is the luminance of the moon and ![][image36] is the brightness of the twilight sky.28 As the moon approaches the horizon, the length of the air column through which its light must travel increases, causing a rapid increase in atmospheric extinction.7 This makes the moon appear fainter even as the sky gets darker.28

The dashboard incorporates this logic into its "Best Time" calculation. Research shows that ![][image14] is directly proportional to site elevation and inversely proportional to moon altitude.28 At moderate elevations (under 1000m), the moon is often first seen just 5 to 10 minutes after sunset, while at high altitudes (around 2000m), the optimal contrast may occur when the moon is as low as 2 degrees above the horizon.28

## **Historical Validation and the HilalPy Framework**

To ensure scientific rigor, the calculations of the dashboard's engine must be validated against historical records of confirmed and failed sightings.32 The Islamic Crescent Observation Project (ICOP) maintains an international database of such reports, dating back to 1998\.34

### **Statistical Analysis via HilalPy**

The HilalPy library provides a specialized interface for analyzing over 8,000 moon sighting data points.33 The dashboard uses this dataset to calculate "Contradiction Rates" for its engine. A negative contradiction occurs when a criterion predicts the moon will not be seen, but it is sighted; a positive contradiction occurs when it is predicted to be visible but remains unseen despite clear skies.33

| Criterion | Mean Error/Contradiction | Ranking (Calendrical Stability) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Odeh (2004) | Low | 1st (Modern Era) |
| MABIMS (2021) | Moderate | 2nd (Regional) |
| Yallop (1997) | Moderate | 3rd (Global) |
| Fotheringham (1910) | High | 12th (Legacy) |

By benchmarking the Skyfield-powered engine against the 8,290 records in the HilalPy database, the dashboard ensures that its "Visibility Probability" score is grounded in empirical reality rather than theoretical abstraction.33 Analysis of this data has identified specific world records, such as the 11 hour and 42 minute old moon sighted via telescope in 2002, which serve as boundary cases for testing the engine's edge-case precision.37

## **The Global Sighting Conflict: Umm al-Qura vs. Local Rukyat**

A primary function of the Hilal Dashboard is the side-by-side comparison of calculations against the Saudi Umm al-Qura calendar. This feature is crucial for many Muslim communities that must navigate the differences between civil administrative calendars and local physical sighting requirements.38

### **The Umm al-Qura Standard**

The Saudi government uses the Umm al-Qura calendar for civil and administrative purposes, based on modern astronomical theories calculated for the coordinates of the Ka'ba in Mecca (![][image37] N, ![][image38] E).40 Since 1423 AH (2002 CE), the calendar has followed two strict astronomical conditions: the geocentric conjunction must occur before sunset in Mecca, and the moon must set after the sun in Mecca.40

| Sighting Phase | Umm al-Qura Requirement | Physical Visibility Requirement |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Conjunction | Before local sunset. | Usually \>15-20 hours before sunset. |
| Horizon Position | Moonset \> Sunset. | ![][image3] typically \> 8-10 degrees. |
| Sighting Status | Month begins by calculation. | Month begins only if hilal is seen. |

Strictly speaking, the Umm al-Qura calendar does not represent the actual visibility of the hilal; in roughly 75% of cases where it starts a month, the crescent is too thin to be seen with the naked eye.40 This often results in Saudi Arabia announcing the start of a month a day earlier than countries that rely on strict local sighting, such as Morocco or Pakistan.43

### **The Limiting Horizon Concept**

The dashboard simulates the "Limiting Horizon" concept to explain these discrepancies. While some regions only look at their own immediate horizon at sunset, the Umm al-Qura system is based on the "certainty" that the moon will be visible *somewhere* in the world before dawn breaks in Mecca.8 This scan moves westward as the clock ticks through the night in Saudi Arabia. If visibility is confirmed in West Africa or South America during this interval, it is considered valid for the announcement in Mecca.8 The dashboard visualizes this "scan" using high-performance GeoJSON map layers, showing exactly where the moon becomes visible relative to the ticking clock in Mecca.8

## **Software Architecture and Technical Stack**

The "Hilal Dashboard" is built as a high-performance web application, leveraging modern Python frameworks for the backend and React/Next.js for the visual layer.46 This choice of technologies ensures the application can handle the high-concurrency demands of a global religious cycle.48

### **Calculation Engine: Skyfield and NumPy**

To ensure scientific rigor, all astronomical calculations are performed locally on the server using **Skyfield**.12 Unlike legacy libraries that often require heavy compilation, Skyfield is written in pure Python and accelerated using **NumPy** vector math.12 This acceleration is critical for generating global visibility heatmaps, as it allows the dashboard to compute visibility variables for a grid of thousands of coordinates (e.g., 1-degree increments of latitude and longitude) in a single vectorized operation.51

The engine's logic follows a specific data pipeline:

1. **Ephemeris Loading:** The dashboard loads the JPL DE421 or DE440 ephemeris files, which cover planet and moon positions with sub-arcsecond accuracy.12  
2. **Coordinate Transformation:** The system transforms geocentric positions into topocentric RA/Dec and then into local Altitude/Azimuth (Alt-Az) using WGS84 ellipsoidal geometry.53  
3. **Refraction Correction:** Alt-Az values are adjusted using the Saemundsson formula, integrated with real-time pressure and temperature data.23  
4. **Criterion Application:** The engine applies the Yallop and Odeh polynomials to the ![][image14] window for each coordinate.3

### **Backend API: FastAPI**

**FastAPI** is selected for the web layer due to its asynchronous (ASGI) architecture and its seamless integration with Python type hints.46 Benchmarks indicate that FastAPI can handle roughly 15,000 to 20,000 requests per second for simple tasks, significantly outperforming synchronous frameworks like Flask.48 For the Hilal Dashboard, this means that while the core calculations are "expensive," the API can manage a massive influx of users during the minutes leading up to Maghrib (sunset) without latency.48

The API exposes endpoints for:

* GET /visibility/{city}: Returning topocentric variables and ![][image12]/![][image21] scores for a specific location.  
* GET /map/geojson: Providing the dynamic visibility bands for the global map.  
* GET /timer/maghrib: Providing high-precision countdowns based on USNO sunrise/sunset definitions (center of sun 50 arcminutes below horizon).55

## **Front-End Experience and Data Visualization**

The "Visual Layer" of the dashboard is designed to be highly interactive, providing users with both a global perspective and a local, "from the garden" view of the horizon.56

### **Interactive Dashboard Design**

The UI is built using **React 18** and **Next.js**, utilizing **Shadcn UI** for its modular design system.47 Shadcn’s "own your components" philosophy is particularly useful for an astronomy app, as it allow for deep customization of input fields for GPS coordinates and atmospheric parameters.59

For animations and micro-interactions, **Framer Motion** is implemented to provide smooth transitions between city search results and the detailed horizon simulator.61 This ensures that the platform remains intuitive for amateur astronomers and religious scholars alike.47

### **Global Mapping and Hardware Acceleration**

To render global visibility heatmaps, the dashboard uses **MapLibre GL JS**, an open-source fork of Mapbox GL JS.64 Unlike standard mapping libraries that rely on DOM manipulation, MapLibre uses **WebGL** technology to offload rendering tasks to the user's GPU.64 This enables the dashboard to display:

* **Dynamic Probability Bands:** Green (Easy), Magenta (Optical Aid Possible), Blue (Optical Aid Required), and Red (Impossible).67  
* **Shadow Terminators:** Real-time day/night cycles and sunset lines.68  
* **Cloud Overlays:** Semi-transparent layers of current cloud cover obtained from the OpenWeatherMap Weather Maps 2.0 API.69

| Feature | Mapping Strategy | Benefit |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Visibility Zones | GeoJSON Polygons | Mathematical precision in probability bands. |
| Sight-ability Score | Weighted Heatmap | Combines ![][image12] value with local cloud cover %. |
| Horizon Simulator | SVG / Canvas | Shows moon's relative position to the setting sun. |
| User Geolocation | Browser API | Instant local prediction based on current coordinates. |

### **The Horizon View Simulator**

The local "Horizon View" is the most nuanced feature of the UI. It provides a vector-based representation of the sky in the minutes following sunset. This simulator accounts for:

* **Lunar Phase Shape:** Dynamically generated ASCII or high-resolution images showing the exact angle and thickness of the crescent.71  
* **Relative Azimuth (![][image5]):** Showing the user exactly how far to the left or right of the sunset position to look for the hilal.18  
* **Refracted Altitude:** Displaying the apparent position of the moon above the local horizon.23

By using image processing techniques like **CLAHE** (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) in the simulation, the dashboard can show users what they might expect to see through a telescope, effectively modeling digital image enhancement.73

## **Meteorological Integration: Cloud Cover and Sight-ability**

Calculated astronomical visibility is of little use if the sky is entirely obscured by clouds.67 The Hilal Dashboard introduces a "Sight-ability Score," which weights the astronomical ![][image12] value against the local fractional cloud cover (![][image39]).74

### **Cloud Detection Algorithms**

The dashboard integrates real-time cloud data from **Tomorrow.io** and **OpenWeatherMap**.76 These APIs provide total cloud cover percentages derived from geostationary satellite imageries and numerical weather prediction models.70 Advanced meteorological research indicates that the transparency of clouds is a function of brightness temperature differences (![][image40]) and optical depth (![][image41]), factors that help distinguish between thin cirrus clouds and opaque storm fronts.79

The weighted score (![][image42]) is calculated as follows:

![][image43]  
Where ![][image44] is the Yallop ![][image12] value mapped to a 0-1 scale, ![][image39] is the cloud cover fraction, and ![][image45] is a constant reflecting the minimum irradiance possible under complete coverage.74

| Cloud Fraction (F) | Sight-ability Reduction | User Recommendation |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 0.0 (Clear) | 0% | Ideal conditions for sighting. |
| 0.5 (Partly Cloudy) | \~10-15% | Sighting possible through "windows" in clouds. |
| 0.87 (Mostly Cloudy) | 50% | Sighting unlikely; optical aid required. |
| 1.0 (Overcast) | \>75% | Physical sighting impossible. |

By combining this score with the prayer countdown timer for Maghrib, the dashboard provides a high-precision countdown to the "Sighting Window," informing users exactly when to be ready with their telescopes or cameras.55

## **Implementation Roadmap and Global Impact**

The deployment of the Hilal Dashboard follows a phased approach, ensuring that the engine is fully validated before the high-concurrency Ramadan cycle.57

### **Phase 1: Engine and Mathematical Validation**

The initial task involves implementing the Yallop and Odeh polynomials in Python using the Skyfield library. This logic is validated against the 8,290 records in the HilalPy dataset to calculate baseline contradiction rates. The output of this phase is a command-line interface (CLI) tool that can generate visibility scores for any city coordinate.33

### **Phase 2: Weather API Integration**

The system connects to the Tomorrow.io and OpenWeatherMap APIs. A hybrid "Sight-ability" algorithm is developed, merging astronomical probability with real-time cloud and atmospheric pressure data to adjust the refraction and contrast values.23

### **Phase 3: Visual Layer and Interactivity**

The sleek dark-mode dashboard is built using Next.js and Shadcn UI. The global visibility heatmaps are rendered via MapLibre GL JS using hardware-accelerated WebGL. The local "Horizon View" is developed as an interactive SVG component that responds to the user's GPS coordinates.59

### **Phase 4: Performance Optimization**

To ensure responsiveness during peak traffic, the system pre-calculates visibility data for major global cities and stores it in a PostgreSQL database. This allows the API to serve most requests with simple database lookups, reserving the expensive local calculations only for custom user-defined GPS coordinates.48

### **Phase 5: Launch and Community Integration**

The final dashboard is deployed via a high-availability provider such as Vercel or AWS. The platform integrates informational links for Zakat al-Fitr and religious education, serving as a unified community resource for the new lunar month.22

## **Future Outlook: Artificial Intelligence in Hilal Observation**

The next evolution of the dashboard lies in the application of Machine Learning (ML) to lunar observation. Recent studies have demonstrated that Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Logistic Regression algorithms, trained on historical ICOP datasets, can achieve a predictive accuracy of over 93%.18 These models can learn the subtle patterns hidden in variables like jet stream speed, humidity gradients, and light pollution that traditional polynomials might overlook.73

By integrating these AI models, the Hilal Dashboard could provide "Personalized Sighting Thresholds," adjusting for an individual observer's location, equipment, and even their visual acuity.11 Furthermore, the application of Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) to live digital feeds from astronomical observatories could allow for the "authentication" of reports in real-time, effectively automating the verification process for religious courts and sighting committees.73

## **Synthesis of Findings and Conclusion**

The Hilal Dashboard represents a paradigm shift from reliance on centralized, often opaque authorities towards a decentralized, scientifically rigorous framework for Islamic timekeeping.1 By synthesizing high-precision astronomical engines with real-time environmental data, the dashboard fulfills the socio-religious requirement of "sighting" while adhering to the technical standards of modern observatories.4

The analysis confirms that the bi-parametric criteria of Yallop and Odeh, when corrected for atmospheric refraction and elevation-dependent best times, provide the most stable foundation for predicting the start of Ramadan and the Eids.3 The integration of live cloud cover data and historical contradiction analysis provides users with a level of clarity that was previously impossible, transforming a traditionally uncertain event into a predictable, data-driven experience.

Ultimately, the Hilal Dashboard is more than a technical tool; it is a catalyst for the global unification of the Islamic calendar.20 By providing a transparent platform where calculation and tradition are displayed side-by-side, it empowers individual communities to reclaim the ancient practice of moon sighting with the power of the 21st century's most advanced computational science.
